title: Firms' Costs of Production
summary: Short run vs long run, explicit and implicit costs, fixed and variable costs, marginal product, law of diminishing returns, and average cost curves.
content_html:
Firms' Costs of Production

Understanding how costs behave is essential for business decision-making. Firms choose their output level by analysing costs and revenues.

Short Run vs Long Run

  • Short run: Period where at least one input is fixed (typically capital/physical plant).
  • Long run: Period long enough for a firm to vary ALL inputs, adopt new technology, and change the size of its plant.

Explicit vs Implicit Costs

  • Explicit costs: Costs that involve spending money (wages, rent, materials).
  • Implicit costs: Non-monetary opportunity costs (e.g. foregone salary from running your own business, foregone interest on invested capital).
  • Economic profit = Total Revenue - (Explicit costs + Implicit costs).
  • Accounting profit = Total Revenue - Explicit costs only.

Fixed and Variable Costs

  • Fixed costs (FC): Do not change as output changes (e.g. rent, loan repayments).
  • Variable costs (VC): Change as output changes (e.g. wages of hourly workers, materials).
  • Total Cost (TC) = FC + VC

Production Function and Marginal Product

Production function: The relationship between inputs employed and maximum output produced.

Marginal product of labour (MPL): The additional output from hiring one more worker.

Law of diminishing returns: At some point, adding more of a variable input (e.g. labour) to the same amount of fixed input (e.g. capital) will cause the marginal product of the variable input to decline.

Key Cost Measures

  • Average Fixed Cost (AFC): FC / Q -- always decreasing as output rises.
  • Average Variable Cost (AVC): VC / Q -- U-shaped curve.
  • Average Total Cost (ATC): TC / Q -- U-shaped; ATC = AFC + AVC.
  • Marginal Cost (MC): Change in TC from producing one more unit. U-shaped; crosses ATC and AVC at their minimum points.

Long-Run Average Cost and Economies of Scale

  • Economies of scale: Long-run average cost falls as output increases.
  • Diseconomies of scale: Long-run average cost rises as output increases.
  • Constant returns to scale: Long-run average cost is constant as output increases.

exercises.title: Short run definition
exercises.prompt: The short run in economics is a period where:
options: All inputs can be varied freely
At least one input is fixed
The firm cannot make any profit
Only one unit of output can be produced
exercises.answer: At least one input is fixed

exercises.title: Implicit cost example
options: A wage payment to an employee
The rent paid for office space
The foregone salary of the owner who manages their own business
exercises.answer: The foregone salary of the owner who manages their own business

exercises.title: Law of diminishing returns
exercises.prompt: The law of diminishing returns states that at some point:
options: Adding more capital increases output indefinitely
Adding more variable input to the same fixed input causes marginal product to decline
exercises.answer: Adding more variable input to the same fixed input causes marginal product to decline

exercises.title: Marginal cost and ATC
exercises.prompt: The marginal cost curve intersects the average total cost curve at:

exercises.title: Economies of scale
options: Long-run average cost rises as output increases
Long-run average cost falls as output increases
Short-run costs are lower than long-run costs
exercises.answer: Long-run average cost falls as output increases

exercises.title: AFC behaviour
exercises.prompt: As output increases, average fixed cost (AFC):

exercises.title: Economic vs accounting profit
exercises.prompt: Economic profit differs from accounting profit because economic profit:
options: Also subtracts implicit (opportunity) costs in addition to explicit costs
Is always higher than accounting profit
exercises.answer: Also subtracts implicit (opportunity) costs in addition to explicit costs
